acceptance_targets: [CH15_CRAFT_CROOK_ROSTER_DRIFT] Pass if a cold reader can map every Crook member introduced at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:7 and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:9 to the same person and role at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:35, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:45, and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:185 without pronoun or name collisions. Fail if Kael still changes gender, Errin still disappears unreconciled, or the jar-carrier/root-chewer identity still shifts with no explanation.

[CH15_CRAFT_DENNA_DEATH_BY_REPORT] Pass if, after reading the revised snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:85 and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:185, a cold reader can say what happened to Denna and name one specific Denna detail the scene makes newly painful. Fail if Denna is still only 'down' or 'dead' with no concrete aftermath beyond the casualty report.

[CH15_AWARD_CAUL_FRACTURE_UNDERLANDED] Pass if a cold reader can point to one exact beat anchored in snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:163, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:175, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:177, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:199, or snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:203 and say, 'This is where Caul stops trusting the System,' naming both the trigger and the resulting action. Fail if the revised chapter still requires Aldric's paraphrase plus general battlefield behavior to infer that break.

[CH15_ROSTER_DEATH_MISMATCH] Ch15's death report names only characters introduced in Ch14. All distinguishing details in the death report match the character who displayed them. Survivor + dead count equals Ch14's total roster.

[CH15_CONSEQUENCE_COMPRESSION] At least one sin-eater death is rendered through concrete sensory detail (body, object, physical reaction) rather than reported in a casualty list. Surviving characters' grief registers through changed behavior or specific physical response, not summary emotion.
[R-07] Chapter 15's opening Thessa description does not use the word 'stained' or reference hand discoloration.
[C-03] All pronoun references to Kael in Chapter 15 use 'he/his/him,' matching Chapter 14.

[C-04] The root-chewing habit belongs to the same named character in both Ch14 and Ch15. The clay jars belong to the same named character in both chapters. No character appears in Ch15 without having been introduced in Ch14.
[length_chapter_15_min_words] Chapter word count is >= 5000 while preserving heading contract.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if a cold reader can map every Crook member introduced at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:7 and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:9 to the same person and role at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:35, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:45, and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:185 without pronoun or name collisions. Fail if Kael still changes gender, Errin still disappears unreconciled, or the jar-carrier/root-chewer identity still shifts with no explanation.
findings.problem: The chapter's opening roster does not reconcile with the provided chapter handoff. In the boundary excerpt, Kael is male and the root-chewer while Errin is the jar-carrier; here Kael becomes a woman at the water barrel, Ruald inherits the roots, and Petra/Denna absorb the other duties with no on-page explanation. Because this scene immediately cashes out who dies and who escapes, the shift reads as continuity corruption, not a deliberate reveal.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rework snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:7, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:9, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:35, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:45, and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:185 so the Crook roster, role assignments, and Kael pronouns match one stable lineup from the opening through the casualty accounting. Either preserve the boundary handoff's Kael/Errin assignments or explicitly establish on page why the personnel have changed before the attack starts, but do not let jar duty, root-chewing, and survivor/dead bookkeeping hop names invisibly.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if, after reading the revised snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:85 and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:185, a cold reader can say what happened to Denna and name one specific Denna detail the scene makes newly painful. Fail if Denna is still only 'down' or 'dead' with no concrete aftermath beyond the casualty report.
findings.problem: One of the chapter's three defining civilian deaths lands almost entirely as report. Denna is introduced with a small human detail, then reduced to 'some kind of pulse' and later a slot in the body count. Because the chapter's engine is that three working sin-eaters die because Maren brought the hunt here, leaving one of those deaths this abstract weakens the chapter's central moral payload.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rework snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:85 and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:185, with support from snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:9 or snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:187, so Denna's loss leaves a concrete on-page trace Maren can actually register: the dropped tally, a visible body position, the pulse's physical residue, or another specific aftermath detail. If Maren cannot witness the hit itself, the aftermath still needs to render Denna as a person lost, not a name transferred into a count.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if a cold reader can point to one exact beat anchored in snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:163, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:175, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:177, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:199, or snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:203 and say, 'This is where Caul stops trusting the System,' naming both the trigger and the resulting action. Fail if the revised chapter still requires Aldric's paraphrase plus general battlefield behavior to infer that break.
findings.problem: The chapter tells us Caul's pursuit is over and implies he has seen the System sacrifice his own party, but the promised specific moment of faith fracture never quite becomes a page-dominating beat. Aldric paraphrases the order and Caul changes objectives; the ideological turn mostly survives as inference. For a chapter whose required consequence is that Caul witnesses the System's true nature and his certainty breaks, that is not enough.
findings.rewrite_direction: Sharpen snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:163, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:175, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:177, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:199, and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:203 so one unmistakable trigger causes one unmistakable behavioral pivot on page. Options: let Caul voice what he has just read, make his turn toward the surviving boy visibly cost him the hunt, or let Maren observe a concrete break in Caul's professional cadence that ties directly to the System ordering his own people expendable.

findings.acceptance_test: Ch15's death report names only characters introduced in Ch14. All distinguishing details in the death report match the character who displayed them. Survivor + dead count equals Ch14's total roster.
findings.evidence: Lines 4057-4059: 'Petra, Ruald, and Denna didn't make it. Kael's alive—barely. Hale too.' Ruald is given the root-chewing trait (line 4059) that Ch14 line 3596 established as Kael's. The continuity sheet's state_transitions for Thessa reference 'Loses Petra, Ruald, and Denna,' suggesting the names were planned but never planted in Ch14's text.
findings.problem: The death report references a planned roster that was never fully written into the preceding chapter. This is a draft-state artifact: the outline knew about six sin-eaters, but the prose only introduced five, and the wrong five. The misattributed root-chewing detail compounds the error by creating a false continuity—implying the reader should remember Ruald chewing root, when in fact Kael was the one who did it.
findings.rewrite_direction: After fixing Ch14's roster (see CH14_INCOMPLETE_ROSTER), revise Ch15 lines 4057-4059 to ensure: (a) every named dead character appeared on-page in Ch14, (b) the root-chewing detail belongs to the character who displayed it in Ch14, (c) the survivor count matches Ch14's roster minus the dead. If Kael chews root in Ch14 and survives in Ch15, do not attribute root-chewing to a dead character in the death report.

findings.acceptance_test: At least one sin-eater death is rendered through concrete sensory detail (body, object, physical reaction) rather than reported in a casualty list. Surviving characters' grief registers through changed behavior or specific physical response, not summary emotion.
findings.evidence: Lines 4057-4059 deliver three sin-eater deaths (Petra, Ruald, Denna) plus aftermath of the chapter's major confrontation in a brief reported-speech montage. The deaths are conveyed as a list item in Thessa's dialogue rather than experienced as events with embodied consequence.
findings.problem: Five chapters (Ch10-14) build toward the Crook confrontation and the sin-eater community's exposure to real danger. The payoff compresses three named deaths into a casualty roster delivered in dialogue, violating the constitution's anti-sanitization mandate (rule 10) and consequence-rendering requirement (rule 12). The reader invests in these characters' world but receives a summary where embodied aftermath should be. This is not restraint intensifying pressure—it is compression evacuating it.
findings.rewrite_direction: Expand lines 4057-4059 into a passage that renders at least one death with sensory presence—a body detail, a survivor's physical reaction, a specific object left behind. The other deaths can land through Thessa's changed behavior or a concrete aftermath detail (not 'she was devastated'). The passage need not be long; a paragraph of embodied specificity outweighs a page of summary. Model: the constitution's grief example (grocery-list detail) rather than the forbidden 'devastated by the loss' pattern.

findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 15's opening Thessa description does not use the word 'stained' or reference hand discoloration.
findings.evidence: Ch15 line 3901: 'her stained hands were empty.' Adjacent to Ch14 which mentions stained hands 4 times (lines 3572, 3596, 3652, 3724).
findings.problem: Thessa's stained hands re-described at Ch15 opening (line 3901), immediately adjacent to Ch14's 4-mention cluster. First established Ch7 line 1637.
findings.rewrite_direction: Replace 'her stained hands were empty' with a different physical detail conveying the same information (she put something down in a hurry). Use posture, expression, or movement instead of staining.

findings.acceptance_test: All pronoun references to Kael in Chapter 15 use 'he/his/him,' matching Chapter 14.
findings.evidence: Ch14 lines 3596-3868: Kael is consistently 'he/his/him' ('The older man finished chewing his root' at 3596; 'Kael said' with 'he' pronouns at 3628, 3822; 'Kael spat his root' at 3868). Ch15 line 3881: 'Kael had just come out of the western shelter, still pulling her coat straight from sleep.'
findings.problem: Kael uses male pronouns throughout Ch14 (he/his/him across 7+ references) and switches to female pronoun ('her coat') at Ch15 line 3881 with no explanation. Adjacent chapters.
findings.rewrite_direction: At line 3881, change 'her coat' to 'his coat' to match Ch14's consistent male pronoun usage for Kael.

findings.acceptance_test: The root-chewing habit belongs to the same named character in both Ch14 and Ch15. The clay jars belong to the same named character in both chapters. No character appears in Ch15 without having been introduced in Ch14.
findings.evidence: Ch14 lines 3628-3868: Kael is the root-chewer (lines 3628, 3676, 3822, 3868: 'Kael spat his root into the dirt'). Ch14 line 3868: Errin gathers clay jars ('Errin stooped to gather her clay jars'). Ch15 line 3879: Ruald is now the root-chewer ('Ruald sat on his upturned bucket... chewing root'); Petra has the clay jars ('Petra had the fire going and three clay jars'). Ch15 lines 3899, 3909, 3927, 3947: Ruald continues with root and dies with it (line 4059: 'Ruald with the root in his cheek'). Errin does not appear in Ch15; Denna and Ruald appear without introduction.
findings.problem: Between Ch14 and Ch15, two character-attribute swaps occur: (1) the root-chewing habit migrates from Kael to the new character Ruald, and (2) the clay jars migrate from Errin to Petra. Additionally, Errin (Ch14) disappears and Denna (Ch15) appears without introduction, suggesting a name change. The death summary at line 4059 follows Ch15's assignments, confirming the errors are in Ch15's reassignment rather than Ch14's establishment.
findings.rewrite_direction: Reconcile the Ch14-Ch15 roster. Either: (A) restore root-chewing to Kael and jars to Errin in Ch15, making Ruald and Denna new characters who are introduced properly; or (B) revise Ch14 to match Ch15's roster if Ruald and Denna are the intended characters. Ensure Errin/Denna naming is consistent. The death summary (line 4059) should match whichever roster is canonical.

findings.acceptance_test: Chapter word count is >= 5000 while preserving heading contract.
findings.rewrite_direction: Add causally necessary scene material that strengthens the chapter engine, increases pressure, and lands the planned state shift without filler.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Preserve productive dialogue roughness; do not clean living speech into balanced thesis statements.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel must commit fully to its own weirdness. A System-blind con man with a dead paladin and a dead assassin arguing in his skull is inherently absurd, and the novel should let that absurdity live alongside genuine grief, genuine violence, and genuine mystery without apologizing for the tonal range. The darkest moments and the funniest moments may occupy the same scene. When they do, let both registers operate simultaneously — do not sacrifice comedy for gravitas or gravitas for comedy. Risk means specificity: the specific sensation of a body doing things you did not tell it to do, the specific comedy of a dead paladin critiquing your hygiene while you're running for your life, the specific horror of carrying incomplete people whose memories have holes, the specific shame of learning that your eleven years of fraud may have killed people. When in doubt between too raw and too safe, choose raw. The novel's emotional range should be wider than any single genre label would suggest.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism in the narration of violence, bodily experience, moral compromise, or physical consequence is a draft-level failure. Maren is a con man who has seen hard things and done dirty work in poor villages for eleven years — his narration should not flinch where he would not flinch. The style bible's sensory bias toward tactile and proprioceptive detail means physical consequence must be rendered through the body: what it feels like to throw up, to kill someone, to have your muscles operate without your consent, to perform a ritual that requires you to open yourself to contamination. Summary and abstraction at the moment of consequence is always worse than presence.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm for all characters except Aldric, whose low contraction level is a deliberate character marker that should erode over the course of the novel. Uncontracted forms in other characters' speech are reserved for emphasis, deliberation, or ceremony. In a world of practical people doing unglamorous work, stiff diction is the exception.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: Alternate between hot and quiet chapters as a structural principle. No two consecutive chapters should open the same way — if one opens with action, the next should open with interiority or setting; if one opens with dialogue, the next should open with physical sensation or observation. Vary scene count between chapters: compressed single-scene chapters after multi-scene ones. Let quiet chapters stay quiet — do not insert action beats or revelations to maintain pace. The pacing engine is Maren's voice and the interplay between the three internal consciousnesses, not external plot pressure.
prose_style_profile.diction: Colloquial with earned elevation. Default register is smart but not literary — a con man's English, precise about people and vague about feelings. Elevation reserved for moments of genuine awe, grief, or the rare glimpse of something beautiful. Fantasy-register portentousness is a draft-level failure — no 'ancient darkness' or 'powers beyond comprehension.' The world is weird and dangerous and ordinary all at once, and the prose should match.
forbidden_drift_patterns: Sentence fragments imitating action-movie or thriller prose — subject-dropping and article-stripping for false urgency
Semicolon-heavy scholarly narration that distances the reader from embodied experience
Present-tense micro-shifts for intensity — the novel is past tense throughout, no exceptions
Filtering words ('he saw,' 'he felt,' 'he noticed,' 'he realized') when direct rendering is available
Fantasy-voice portentousness: 'ancient,' 'dark power,' 'something stirred,' 'a presence beyond reckoning'
Comic narration that winks at the reader or breaks the fourth wall instead of committing to the situation
Repeated metaphor templates — if a comparison structure appears twice in a chapter, the second instance is a tic
Emotional summary replacing scene presence: 'He was overwhelmed by grief' instead of rendering what grief looks and feels like in the body
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person, locked to Maren throughout. Aldric and Vess are experienced as internal voices — they speak as italicized dialogue within Maren's consciousness, clearly distinguished from his own thoughts. Maren cannot access their thoughts directly; he hears what they say to him, not what they think. The narration inherits Maren's sensory limitations: the System interface is always mediated through Aldric or Vess's descriptions, never rendered directly until the single exception in Chapter 17.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Varied by emotional register. Default: medium-length sentences with periodic short declaratives for grounding. When Maren is performing (lying, charming, faking): smoother, more controlled rhythm with longer subordinate-clause chains. When Maren is honest or overwhelmed: choppier, more fragmented, more concrete. Fight scenes: visceral and slightly disoriented, reflecting Maren's System-blindness — sensation and proprioception without the visual data everyone else has. Quiet scenes: allow longer, more observational sentences. The rhythm should breathe differently in every chapter.
sensory_bias: tactile — Maren's primary sense channel; the physical world is how he navigates System-blindness
auditory — the hum of System activity, Aldric and Vess's voices, the specific sounds of quest-light and contamination
proprioceptive — Maren's body doing things he didn't choose; the internal sensation of carrying fragments; the physical cost of consumption
olfactory — contamination has smell; ritual has smell; the world stinks in specific ways that the System-sighted don't notice because they navigate by interface
